macro_segments.title: Review of Yesterday's Principles: Heter Mitztaref L'Issur and K'zayis B'chdei Achilas Pras

micro_segments.title: Introduction to Heter Mitztaref L'Issur and its application to Nazir with bread soaked in wine
Distinction between Heter Mitztaref L'Issur and the principle that forbidden things in mixtures retain their status; the case of Kutach HaBavli on Pesach
K'zayis B'chdei Achilas Pras principle: eating forbidden food within the time it takes to eat a loaf of bread regardless of mixture
Application of K'zayis B'chdei Achilas Pras to grain mixed with grain (not distinct) and the halakhic rule that bittul does not occur when the forbidden item gives off taste

macro_segments.title: Introduction to Taam K'Ikar: The Principle That Taste Counts Like the Thing Itself

micro_segments.title: Definition of Taam K'Ikar and the two scenarios: pure taste (Taamo V'Lo Mamasho) and dissolved substance (Taamo U'Mamasho)
Two underlying ideas behind Taam K'Ikar: taste as indication of presence and prevention of bittul
Conceptual distinction between transgressing for the actual quantity of forbidden food versus a new issur of taste (Issur Ta'am)

macro_segments.title: Abaye's Challenge: Is Mishrat About Heter Mitztaref or Taam K'Ikar?

micro_segments.title: Abaye questions whether Mishrat Anavim teaches Heter Mitztaref L'Issur or the broader principle of Taam K'Ikar

macro_segments.title: Derasha from Mishrat Anavim: Establishing Taam K'Ikar from Torah

micro_segments.title: Textual basis from Mishrat Anavim: grapes soaked in water taking on wine taste, establishing principle applicable to all prohibitions in Torah
A fortiori argument from Nazir (lenient prohibition) to all other prohibitions (more stringent)
Distinction between the derasha teaching Taam K'Ikar (taste counts) versus Heter Mitztaref L'Issur (combining bread and wine amounts)

macro_segments.title: Abaye's Challenge: Is Mishrat About Heter Mitztaref or Taam K'Ikar? (continued)

micro_segments.title: Response that Heter Mitztaref L'Issur is limited to Nazir according to Rabbi Akiva, while Taam K'Ikar is the general principle

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Akiva's Source for Taam K'Ikar: From Kelei Midian Rather Than Basar B'Chalav

micro_segments.title: Basar B'Chalav as potentially demonstrating Taam K'Ikar, but rejected because the prohibition requires cooking, not mere mixture of taste
Kelei Midian and the transfer of taste from non-kosher vessels: establishing that absorbed taste counts as the forbidden thing itself
Debate whether Kelei Midian represents a general principle of Taam or a special halakha unique to vessels; discussion of Ta'am Le-Pagam (taste that worsens)
Ben Yomo concept: vessels forbidden only on the day of use because taste is not yet spoiled; connection to identity of vessel versus absorbed taste

macro_segments.title: Why Rabbanan Learn Taam K'Ikar from Mishrat Rather Than Kelei Midian

micro_segments.title: Rabbanan position: Kelei Midian is a special halakha not fitting normal taste rules, so cannot serve as basis for general principle
Rabbanan learn Taam K'Ikar from Mishrat and apply it broadly to all Torah prohibitions via the principle of Kol HaTorah Kulah

macro_segments.title: Two Torah Verses on Mixture Principles: Nazir and Chatas, and the Debate Over Their Scope

micro_segments.title: The principle of Shnei Ketuvim Habaim K'Echad: when a rule appears in two places, it indicates exceptions rather than a general rule
Source of Heter Mitztaref L'Issur in Chatas: absorbing of sacrificial flesh and the resulting obligation to treat absorbed object by stringencies of the original
Rabbi Akiva's reading: Nazir and Chatas teach Heter Mitztaref L'Issur (limited to these two), while Rabbanan read Mishrat in Nazir as teaching Taam K'Ikar (general principle)
Rabbanan's counter-argument: Chatas is unique to Kodashim (stringencies not learned from Kodashim to regular prohibitions), while Mishrat in Nazir applies everywhere
Resolution: Rabbanan hold Taam K'Ikar from Mishrat applies universally; Rabbi Akiva limits Heter Mitztaref L'Issur to Nazir and Chatas but learns Taam K'Ikar from Kelei Midian
